text:
Fall intake guys take computer security instead of Data Models and Query lang and even those who are good at coding are swamped with assignments for weeks on end which take a ton of time to do. Spring with a shorter semester and probably a bit heavier course compared to computer security… All the best but its going to be a bit tough

I don't know how you can take algorithms or intro to ML without having a firm footing in coding.

Just curious, how are you just getting into coding and are still able to take these classes?

Cs department is filled with great profs

Coming from an average student freshman year, even the hw assignments took me days of nonstop work to complete, and don’t even get me started on the labs and projects.

If you’re not prepared to spend every waking hour working on assignments, you won’t do well, or even pass for that matter. That is, assuming you are in fact an “average” or below average student.

Like all programs, it is what you make of it. You can squeeze shit tons of values out of UB CS program if you put in the effort for good grades, network/maintain a relationship with your professors and peers(associate with the well-connected and successful ones, not the wishy-washy types) and take advantage of all the resources and opportunities the campus has on offer. this includes clubs, office hours, tutoring, researches, events, workshops, careers centers, and ect. I mean you or someone else has paid for these so take full advantage of it. Otherwise it would like be like you paying for Netflix subscription but never watch a single video on it.
Also, look for opportunities outside of

it. Otherwise it would like be like you paying for Netflix subscription but never watch a single video on it.
Also, look for opportunities outside of school. Studies on college outcomes often said the school does not matter as much as what you study and what you do. I fully subscribe to that. Of course, dont go to shitty school with 80+% drop out rates that barely invest anything into their students, that should be common sense. With that said, UB is pretty solid for a good value so you shouldnt need to worry about this.

1. not sure, colleges are very much none transparent about this data and for a good reason, it is bad marketing. I suggest you go to the college board(or was it DoE?) which

very much none transparent about this data and for a good reason, it is bad marketing. I suggest you go to the college board(or was it DoE?) which seems to have data for alumni careers, salary, and ect 5 year post-graduation for anyone who took out federal loans. When i was a student, i did not bother with career fairs, you are competing against countless other students. you will be much better off just applying for internships or job opportunities on indeed or linkedin. Edit: found the link. https://collegescorecard.ed.gov/school/?196088-University-at-Buffalo
2. very large for freshmen years but classes will get much smaller into junior and senior years because many get weed out or dropped

2. very large for freshmen years but classes will get much smaller into junior and senior years because many get weed out or dropped the program.
3. yes but like i said, only if you take full advantage of it. you can coast and be lazy and still pass with a c but you wont learn much and wont be any better off compared to lesser ranked school with a bad program. If you are diligent, you will learn a lot and be extremely knowledgable.

The CS program is awesome, the faculty is definitely good, but of course there are some bad professors.

As far as extracurriculars, we have plenty. Seconding the other commenter who said that things outside the department like SEDS or AIAA or nanosat are better for the actual technical stuff. Networking wise, we've got DivTech (formerly scientista), ACM, oSTEM (more generally for LGBT people in any stem major), and if you're looking for work, there is a career fair, but it's very defense heavy for CS and engineering.

The career fair is doghit. There's literally no big tech based companies like Google, Facebook,etc. No NYC fintech companies like Morgan Stanley, Bloomberg,etc and definitely

There's literally no big tech based companies like Google, Facebook,etc. No NYC fintech companies like Morgan Stanley, Bloomberg,etc and definitely no silicon valley startups. All you get are some defense companies and some other local companies.

CSE 220 and CSE 250 are weed out courses, each with a (alleged) fail/ dropout rate of 50% so be extra vigilant when taking these courses.

Try to avoid taking physics. Safe to say vast majority of students don't speak highly about the physics department.

Don't even try to cheat. You WILL get caught and you WILL get bent over. Know multiple students who didn't take the warning seriously, cheated on some trivial hw that barely counted for overall

and you WILL get bent over. Know multiple students who didn't take the warning seriously, cheated on some trivial hw that barely counted for overall grade, got caught and got an F on the entire course and have to retake it. If they cheat again they'll get kicked out of the major.

Unpopular opinion but I'd say the vast majority of software engineering based clubs like ACM, DivTech, etc. are a fairly useless avenue for learning tech related skills. They're pretty good for networking though. But I'd say spend some time hopping around different clubs and stick with whatever you feel is worthwhile. Personally I felt AIAA Micro G and UB Nanosat to be the most worthwhile as they work under strict

and stick with whatever you feel is worthwhile. Personally I felt AIAA Micro G and UB Nanosat to be the most worthwhile as they work under strict deadlines so there's always something going on.

Apply externally (and no I don't mean Handshake it's doghit too apply directly on the company website). So getting an internship is relatively simple (definitely not easy, but simple).

Starting off you're not gonna have proper work experience so make some coding projects to put on your resume. Don't make generic shit like tic tac toe or Todo lists.

Know how to write a good resume. I'd recommend overleaf templates. Use metrics wherever possible (like "sped up this by 50%","contributed 500 lines of

how to write a good resume. I'd recommend overleaf templates. Use metrics wherever possible (like "sped up this by 50%","contributed 500 lines of code",etc). Always state what technologies you worked with.

Apply early. General applications for Summer 2022 internships will start opening up en masse around July and August of 2021.

Apply a lot it's a numbers game. Applied to 250+ places before landing my first internship.

Grind leetcode. Self explanatory, your resume doesn't get you the job it merely gets you an interview. And to Ace technical interviews leetcode is a must.

Get better at behavioral interviews. Somewhat hard to get better at. Just gotta fail some in the beginning and learn

interviews leetcode is a must.

Get better at behavioral interviews. Somewhat hard to get better at. Just gotta fail some in the beginning and learn from those.

Hey there! I've done 2 internships during my time at UB (4 overall). My experience has been mixed. It depends a lot on the company you're aiming for.

If it's a local small-medium company, you don't need much leetcode. Just something that demonstrates competency

If it's a startup, they value relevant projects way more than how you performed grade wise. If you're interested in startups, start learning langs like js, ts, and python. Learn libs like React or Vue and build cool stuff with them (an example being a game marketplace like steam, since you mentioned making games in the past)

For unicorns and FAANG/FAANG-adjacent, it's a mix of projects and being able to do leetcode questions. The

you mentioned making games in the past)

For unicorns and FAANG/FAANG-adjacent, it's a mix of projects and being able to do leetcode questions. The UB courses are not nearly enough, you have to do as many projects and leetcode questions as you can if you want to ace the online assessments (OAs). Companies are starting to increase the difficulty a lot. I recommend neetcode.io and doing the neetcode 150. It takes almost a month to even be ready to tackle leetcode mediums confidently, let alone hards. If you start this in your sophomore year and consistently do leetcode, you're almost guaranteed to ace the OAs for FAANG companies.

It's a great idea to do the UB Hacking hackathon. Don't stop

consistently do leetcode, you're almost guaranteed to ace the OAs for FAANG companies.

It's a great idea to do the UB Hacking hackathon. Don't stop there, do hackathons all over the country, do HackPrinceton, HackMIT, as many as you can. Recruiters sponsor these events and have tables, and if you build impressive stuff then you could even get a guaranteed interview. Go to clubs like ACM as well. Lots of good career advice stuff there.

Neither startups nor FAANG care much about non relevant work experience. Start using things like ELN at UB for more work experience.
For cse 116 I’d take Jesse if you like being taught with lecture slides or Paul if you prefer live coding. Both are really great professors though and you can’t go wrong with either one.

Advisors can be very cynical and having experienced that for other reasons I know that it's discouraging as hell, but I'll take that before advisors over promising and students getting pulled in to expensive degrees they can't use/finish.

With that being said, if you are truly willing to commit to getting the degree. You may want to temper your expectations on getting a STEM degree in 3 years if your previous degree really doesn't contribute credits to the CS degree. Overextending yourself is the best way to burn out and put yourself further behind instead of getting ahead. This is especially true if you also need to work to support yourself. 

I say go to a community college and then do a

instead of getting ahead. This is especially true if you also need to work to support yourself. 

I say go to a community college and then do a 2+2 with a 4 year school. The cs program at ub is extremely challenging and majority of students change majors or drop out. I made it through 3 semesters and then went to a community college and then a bachelor's with a school affiliated with them and i wish that's where i started.

The value of CS is up to you. Yeah it is said CS is more valuable for its rigorous learning and teaches the abstracts of computers and computer programming, however any other technology adjacent degree gets you the same job placement as them.

ITM here is required to take

and computer programming, however any other technology adjacent degree gets you the same job placement as them.

ITM here is required to take Cybersecurity courses, so you don’t have to take the minor to get access to the classes and it’s more network and database focused compared to the CS degree, which is more so you want to do anyways. The CS degree is just highly in-depth problem involving math and programming, which in itself is valuable for any industry

Personally, I’ll test the waters with CS, see if I like it or not, more so if can I tolerate the general Ed’s when I enjoy my cs classes. If you like it, great see it to the next semester, if not move on to the next major of interest.

the general Ed’s when I enjoy my cs classes. If you like it, great see it to the next semester, if not move on to the next major of interest. There’s always a resource for doing a CS degree so definitely reach out.

I'm also a transfer "adult learner" who was out of school for 6 years with an Associate degree in something completely different. While I do believe the CS program is rigorous at UB, I think it's completely doable for someone who dedicates themselves to learning and completing the material in class on time. Coming from someone who has a degree from a community college already, and now being at UB, the classes at UB are definitely harder but I think that just comes with the

who has a degree from a community college already, and now being at UB, the classes at UB are definitely harder but I think that just comes with the territory of UB being a university.

I have the same plan of taking summer classes and graduating in 3 years and I seem to be on track for that as of right now while working part time in the mornings and taking classes from 1pm-6pm on average. I would look into seeing what type of transfer credits you can apply to your degree, even if they're just gen eds and then making a plan in the UB Hub planner to see how many semesters it might take to complete the rest of the program requirements. If you don't have access to that yet, take a look at the

to see how many semesters it might take to complete the rest of the program requirements. If you don't have access to that yet, take a look at the CS program page on the UB website and pick and choose from there. If you have access to the SEAS Academics, even better. Go there and look at their optimized planner for the next few years and see if you can handle that (though that does not include summer semesters so you'll have to figure that out on your own).

I can't speak on anything to do with IT since that is not what I am focusing on, but if the goal is a CS degree specifically I think UB is a great school and in a much better location than Buff State and ECC.

I’m a non-cs major who had to take CSE115, and I won’t sugarcoat it, this course was ROUGH. The professors are fantastic, especially Hertz, but I always felt behind. Ultimately this is a comp sci class, not a programming class, so a lot of this is utilizing the language you learned in class to solve problems, not just learning the language.
For example, in class you might learn how to use Python to sort a list of words in alphabetical order, and how to concatenate a chunk of text to make a list separated by commas. Then on an exam you might be asked to take a chunk of text, turn that into a list of words, and then sort that in alphabetical order. It’s just applying the functions you learn in

to take a chunk of text, turn that into a list of words, and then sort that in alphabetical order. It’s just applying the functions you learn in class, what’s important is remembering the “grammar,” memorizing the “vocabulary,” and being able to write sentences. It’s a lot like learning a language.
I won’t lie: 22 credits is a LOT, and you might find that the majority of your time will go into this class. It’s smart to want to prep before the semester starts. I’ll tell you that, as far as I know, Python is the primary focus of this class. However, learning the language doesn’t improve your problem solving abilities, which are the main deal here. Anybody can memorize the functions, what

However, learning the language doesn’t improve your problem solving abilities, which are the main deal here. Anybody can memorize the functions, what separates people who pass from people who fail is being able to apply those logically. If you do plan on learning some python, definitely make sure you’re practicing the functional uses of functions with problems and data.

Honestly I don’t even recommend trying to prepare for 191. I think math is much harder to self teach. Just put in effort when you take it and you’ll be fine.
For 116, I would recommend familiarizing yourself with Java syntax. And then learn concepts of OOP like classes and polymorphism. If you have time to dig deeper before the spring semester, watch some videos about data structures like linked lists, trees, and graphs. Learn about some kinds of algorithms for traversing those data structures so you can get a feel for algorithmic thinking.

If you have Nasrin for 191, just always attend your recitations (seriously, your TA will sometimes guide you all the way through questions) and for 116,

you have Nasrin for 191, just always attend your recitations (seriously, your TA will sometimes guide you all the way through questions) and for 116, just play around with basic Java and try to familiarize yourself with IntelliJ -- the IDE that class uses.

If you're really looking to thoroughly prepare ahead of time, online tutorials on Java and Discrete Structures are probably safe resources for both classes, respectively. During the semester:

116: make a point to grind hard on projects, attend OH liberally, reread lecture slides before interviews/quizzes

191: write things down carefully, use Google, take smaller assignments seriously to rack up points

116: get Jesse for teachers, learn

191: write things down carefully, use Google, take smaller assignments seriously to rack up points

116: get Jesse for teachers, learn java, go to class, take notes. Optionally learn basic data structures (list, array)

191: get Nasrin for teachers, go to class, take notes. Optionally learn the basics of set theory and graphs, as well as how to do basic proofs.

One of these classes probably talks about big O notation but I'm not sure which. But if you just came out of 115 (which I have not taken at UB so I am not sure of the content) you probably don't need to study up about it before hand, or rather, you don't need to study anything really.

It's extremely difficult and complicated because UB/SEAS wants it to be extremely difficult and complicated

I've been a TA for many Cs undergrad courses and these things are designed that way

CSE 250 is the main filter course for CS graduates...

Everyone who has good basics, solid fundamentals and is willing to put in the crazy work and keep up with the variety of tests/assignments gets to go ahead and take junior, senior level classes (for most of which this is a pre req) tbh

And those who can't step away and end up changing their paths while they still have time to do so and graduate in other courses

The dropout rate AFTER clearing 250 is almost negligible for CS students so basically

they still have time to do so and graduate in other courses

The dropout rate AFTER clearing 250 is almost negligible for CS students so basically anyone who can clear this barrier is usually good enough to tackle the rest as well

Whereas the ones who can't clear 250 usually end up switching out completely and shifting their majors outside CS.

Sure there might be a few talented CS hopefuls who may not be able to contend with the huge workload that Hughes pushes on them but the department seems to think that's an acceptable risk to having a course that can filter students properly.

Hughes could/should definitely stop teaching other classes and put his entire focus on making 250 better but

that can filter students properly.

Hughes could/should definitely stop teaching other classes and put his entire focus on making 250 better but it is always going to be extremely difficult and with a huge workload
